title: Managing Experimental Data: Outliers, Distribution & Normality
summary: Lecture 6. The first job of a metrologist facing a cloud of measurements is to **clean** it and **characterize** it. You will learn the descriptive statistics ($m_x$, $s_x$, quartiles, box-plot), how to spot a *measurement accident* with **Chauvenet's criterion** and the **IQR / box-plot** rule, how to build a **frequency distribution** and histogram, and how to decide whether the data are **normal** — rigorously with the **$\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test** and graphically with the **Normal Probability Plot (NPP)**. A non-normal distribution is the fingerprint of a hidden *systematic factor*.
objectives: Compute the position index $m_x$ and the dispersion index $s_x$ (sample standard deviation, STDEV.S) of a measurement set, and read its quartiles / box-plot.
Identify a measurement accident with Chauvenet's criterion: per-datum risk $\alpha=1/(2n)$, limits $m\mp z\,s$ with $z=\mathrm{NORM.S.INV}(1-1/(4n))$.
Apply the distribution-free IQR / box-plot rule with fences $Q_1-1.5\,IQR$ and $Q_3+1.5\,IQR$.
Build a frequency distribution: number of classes $n_c=\sqrt{n}+4$, examined field $\bar x\pm4s$, class width = field / $n_c$, and the histogram of frequency densities.
Run a $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit test for normality: statistic $W=\sum (f_a-f_t)^2/f_t$, degrees of freedom = categories $-3$, two-sided accept/reject band.
Read a Normal Probability Plot: plotting position $f_{rc,i}=(i-0.5)/n$, quantile $z_i$, linearity $\Rightarrow$ normal; recognize hypernormal and hyponormal shapes.

blocks.text: 1. Why we manage the data first

A measurement result is **biased** (owing to *systematic effects*), **hazy** (owing to *random effects*), and may contain **measurement accidents** (a misread, a vibration, a chip). The user of the *measurement complex* must extract from this *experimental cloud* a single **value** for the measurand and its **uncertainty**.

The purpose of the analysis of experimental results is therefore to identify a **position index** (where the data sit) and a **dispersion index** (how spread they are), *after* implementing statistical techniques that detect — and let us remove — **systematic effects** and **measurement accidents**.

blocks.title: The whole pipeline
blocks.text: **1.** Compute $m_x$ and $s_x$ → **2.** detect & treat outliers (Chauvenet / IQR) → **3.** build the frequency distribution and test normality with $\chi^2$ → **4.** confirm graphically with the NPP. The procedure is **iterative**: the reference normal uses $m$ and $s$, but these are themselves distorted by accidents/systematic effects, so removing the most marked effect and recomputing **improves the sensitivity** of every later comparison.
2. Descriptive statistics: position and dispersion

blocks.title: Mean and sample standard deviation
blocks.caption: $m_x$ is the **position index** (Excel AVERAGE), $s_x$ the **dispersion index**. Note the **$n-1$** at the denominator: a finite set is a *sample* of the underlying process, so we use STDEV.S, never STDEV.P (which divides by $n$ and underestimates the spread).

blocks.text: The $i$-th **percentile** is the value below which $i$ percent of the observations fall. The three quartiles are $Q_1$ = 1st quartile = 25th percentile, $Q_2$ = median = 50th percentile, $Q_3$ = 3rd quartile = 75th percentile (Excel QUARTILE). A **box-plot** draws a box from $Q_1$ to $Q_3$ with the median inside, and whiskers to the min and max. Comparing the box-plots of several operators reveals at a glance whether some groups sit systematically higher than others.

$IQR=Q_3-Q_1$ is the width of the central 50% of the data. Because quartiles ignore the extreme tails, the IQR is a **robust** measure of spread — a single wild value cannot inflate it the way it inflates $s_x$.
3. Exclusion principles — Chauvenet's criterion

An **outlier** is a datum affected significantly by *additional or different factors* than the bulk of the data. It may change the conclusions of an experiment — but it also often carries important information about the process. **Before** excluding anything, understand the possible cause and judge whether similar values may recur: the exclusion must not be decided on a *purely statistical* basis; **expert opinion is always required**.

Chauvenet's method connects the probability of a measurement accident with the **number of tests** $n$. Chauvenet assigned a **conventional probability of 50%** to the event of having one outlier among $n$ tests. Modelling this with a binomial distribution:

blocks.title: Chauvenet's exclusion principle
blocks.caption: $p$ is the probability of a measurement accident in a *single* test (supposed small). Setting the expected number of accidents to $\tfrac12$ gives the per-datum risk $p=1/(2n)$.

blocks.title: Exclusion limits (two-tailed)
blocks.caption: The risk $p$ is split symmetrically between the two tails ($1/4n$ each). Any datum falling outside $[\,m-z\,s,\;m+z\,s\,]$ is judged an outlier. Equivalently the limits are $\mathrm{NORM.INV}(\tfrac{1}{4n};m;s)$ and $\mathrm{NORM.INV}(1-\tfrac{1}{4n};m;s)$.

blocks.title: The risk shrinks as n grows
blocks.text: Because $\alpha=1/(2n)$ depends on $n$, the percentage of data legitimately belonging to the tails *increases* with the number of tests: the more data you have, the more extreme a point must be before it counts as an accident. Report $\alpha$ as a **probability** (e.g. $n=20\Rightarrow\alpha=1/40=0.025$), **not** as a percentage, and always use $s$ from **STDEV.S**.

blocks.term: What to do with an identified outlier
blocks.text: Three options, in order of preference: (a) **repeat** the measurement and replace the outlier with the new (replacement) datum; (b) if it cannot be repeated and there is no replacement, simply **eliminate** it; (c) if a missing value cannot be tolerated, substitute it with the **average of the remaining data** — a trick that barely shifts the mean but may distort the variance and the distribution shape, so it is the last resort.
4. The IQR / box-plot (Tukey) rule

blocks.title: Box-plot fences
blocks.caption: A distribution-free alternative to Chauvenet: any datum beyond these fences is flagged. For a normal distribution the $1.5\,IQR$ fences sit at about $\pm2.7\sigma$. Being built on quartiles, the rule is **robust** — one extreme point cannot move the fences the way it moves $m$ and $s$.

blocks.text: 5. The frequency distribution and the histogram
We bin the data into **classes** (categories, bins) to see the shape of the distribution. There is no strict standard for the number of classes; the course uses:

blocks.title: Classes, examined field, class width
blocks.caption: The **examined field** is widened to $\bar x\pm4s$ so it covers essentially all of the reference normal. Too many classes leaves the core riddled with empty bins; too few hides the shape. Pick a **starting value** that matches no datum and a width at the data resolution, to avoid ambiguity on class boundaries.

blocks.text: The **absolute frequency** $f_a$ is the count of data in a class. The **relative frequency** $f_r=f_a/n$ estimates the probability of that class; the $f_r$ sum to 1 and equal the **areas** of the histogram bars. The bar **heights** are the **frequency densities** $\rho_f=f_r/\Delta x$. Because the areas sum to one, the histogram is directly comparable with a probability density — specifically the **normal distribution** with parameters $m$ and $s$ (the sample estimates).

blocks.title: Bar plot is not a distribution
blocks.text: A **bar chart** whose heights are the *absolute* frequencies $f_a$ does **not** represent a probability distribution (its total area is $n\Delta x$, not 1). Only the **histogram** of frequency *densities* $\rho_f$ has unit area and can be overlaid on the normal curve.
6. Normality tests — the chi-square goodness-of-fit

If the variations are due **only to random effects**, the expected distribution is **normal**. If the experimental distribution is **not** normal, we may assume the presence of **systematic factors**. Two tests check the hypothesis of normality: the **$\chi^2$ test** (rigorous, numeric, lets us *reject* at a confidence level $P$) and the **Normal Probability Plot** (graphic; cannot reject rigorously, but shows the *cause* of non-normality).

blocks.title: The chi-square statistic
blocks.caption: $f_a$ = observed (absolute) count in class $j$; $f_t$ = **theoretical** count predicted by the normal, obtained from the difference of the cumulative normal $F_N$ (Excel NORM.DIST(...;1)) at the class limits times $n$. $W$ has a Pearson $\chi^2$ distribution with $N-3$ degrees of freedom.

blocks.title: Degrees of freedom = categories − 3
blocks.text: $\nu=(\text{number of categories})-(\text{imposed constraints})$. **Three** constraints are imposed when matching the normal to the histogram: equal **mean**, equal **standard deviation**, and equal **total area** (unitary). So $\nu=N-3$. Forgetting the $-2$ for the two fitted parameters is the classic mistake — it changes the critical $\chi^2$ and your conclusion.

steps.text: Choose the confidence level $P$ by weighing the consequence of **rejecting a true** normality (a type-I error). Re-examining the method is cheap, so a high risk $\alpha=15\%$, i.e. $P=85\%$, is acceptable.
Compute $W$ from Eq. 6.6 and the degrees of freedom $\nu=N-3$.

The test is **two-sided (bilateral)**: get a *lower* and an *upper* $\chi^2$ boundary for $P$. Accept normality (no detectable systematic effect) if $W$ falls **inside** the band; reject if it falls **outside**.

Worked figures (length example): $N=11$ categories, $\nu=8$, $P=85\%$ give boundaries $3.14$ and $14.27$. Since $W=10.94$ lies inside, the hypothesis of normality is **not rejected** — though small disturbances may remain undetected.

blocks.title: Why two-sided?
blocks.text: We reject not only when $W$ is **too high** (distribution too different from normal) but also when $W$ is **too low** (distribution suspiciously *too similar* to a perfect normal). A near-perfect fit is desirable but very unlikely, and therefore suspected — it can signal a *filtered* dataset.
7. Normality tests — the Normal Probability Plot (NPP)

The NPP deforms the ordinate scale so that the S-shaped cumulative normal becomes a **straight line**. If the data are normal, their points fall on that line; deviations reveal the *type* of non-normality.

steps.text: Assign each the cumulative plotting position $f_{rc,i}=\dfrac{i-0.5}{n}$. The $-0.5$ is a continuity correction: it keeps the largest value off $f=1$ (where $z=+\infty$).
Convert to the standard-normal quantile $z_i=\mathrm{NORM.S.INV}(f_{rc,i})$, and the reference value $x_{Norm,i}=\bar x+z_i\,s$.

Plot the experimental points $\{x_i;z_i\}$ against the reference line $\{x_{Norm,i};z_i\}$. A **rectilinear** trend (within a band sized by the random irregularities) means normality cannot be rejected; the slope of the line is $\propto 1/s$ and it crosses $z=0$ at $\bar x$.

blocks.term: Reading the shape: hypernormal vs hyponormal
blocks.text:
- **Hypernormal**: the bell is *higher* in the centre and *lower* in the tails; the NPP has a steeper central slope and flattens in the tails. Metrologically it signals a **filtering** process — the operator personally discarded the data farthest from the expected value, distorting the distribution.
- **Hyponormal**: the bell is *lower* in the centre and *heavier* in the tails (often bimodal: the NPP's two tails tend to straight lines). It points to **systematic differences** — *discontinuities* (different instruments, machines, operators) or *tendencies* (temperature change, wear, drift in time).

blocks.title: What a failed normality test tells you
blocks.text: $\chi^2$ and NPP **detect** a systematic factor but do not **identify** it. A hyponormal/bimodal shape hints at discontinuities or a drift — exactly the situation that the next lecture (linear regression) and ANOVA are built to pin down.

question.prompt: A set of $n=18$ readings is screened with Chauvenet's criterion. Which expression gives the standardized cut $z$ used for the exclusion limits $m\mp z\,s$?
question.explanation: The per-datum risk is $\alpha=p=1/(2n)$, split symmetrically so each tail carries $1/(4n)$. The upper cut corresponds to cumulative probability $1-1/(4n)$, hence $z=\mathrm{NORM.S.INV}(1-1/(4n))$. **A** uses the *total* two-sided risk $1/(2n)$ in one tail, cutting too close. **C** returns the negative lower-tail $z$ (and uses the un-halved risk). **D** is the IQR box-plot fence offset, a different rule entirely (and it is a distance, not a $z$).
question.theory: Chauvenet: $\alpha=1/(2n)$ total, $1/(4n)$ per tail, $z=\mathrm{NORM.S.INV}(1-1/(4n))$, limits $m\mp z\,s$.

practice.prompt: Which Excel function gives the dispersion index $s_x$ that should be used for a finite set of experimental data?
practice.explanation: Experimental data are a **sample** of the underlying process, so the dispersion index uses Bessel's correction, dividing by $n-1$: **STDEV.S**. **A** (STDEV.P) divides by $n$ and is only correct for a complete population — it underestimates the spread and is the classic trap. **B** is a variance (squared, and the population form). **C** is the mean absolute deviation, a different statistic the course does not use here.
practice.theory: Sample statistics use $n-1$ degrees of freedom because one is spent estimating the mean; $s_x=\sqrt{\sum(x_i-m_x)^2/(n-1)}$.

practice.prompt: In Chauvenet's criterion the per-datum risk of wrongly excluding a value, and its split between the tails, are:

options.content: $\alpha=0.05$ regardless of $n$, split as $0.025$ per tail

practice.explanation: Chauvenet sets the expected number of accidents in $n$ tests to $\tfrac12$, giving $np\approx\tfrac12$, i.e. $\alpha=p=\tfrac{1}{2n}$; working two-sided each tail carries half, $\tfrac{1}{4n}$. **A** doubles the risk and ignores the symmetric split. **B** is the fixed $5\%$ of a generic hypothesis test, not Chauvenet's $n$-dependent value. **D** halves $\alpha$ and forgets the two-sided split. For $n=20$: $\alpha=1/40=0.025$, $1/80=0.0125$ per tail.
practice.theory: Chauvenet's risk shrinks with sample size: with more data, a point must be more extreme before it is judged an accident.

practice.prompt: A frequency distribution is built for $n=50$ data using the course rule. The number of classes $n_c$ is:
practice.explanation: The course uses $n_c=\sqrt{n}+4=\sqrt{50}+4\approx 7.07+4=11.07\to 11$ classes, with the examined field widened to $\bar x\pm4s$. **A** uses only $\sqrt{n}$, the bare starting point before the $+4$ widening. **C** and **D** are different binning conventions not used here. The $+4$ extra classes ensure the field covers the significant range of the reference normal.
practice.theory: Class scheme: $n_c=\sqrt{n}+4$ classes spanning the field $\bar x\pm4s$; class width $\Delta x=(\max_f-\min_f)/n_c$.

practice.prompt: A $\chi^2$ goodness-of-fit to a normal uses 11 categories; both the mean and the standard deviation were estimated from the data. The number of degrees of freedom is:
practice.explanation: Three constraints are imposed when matching the normal to the histogram — equal total area, equal mean, equal standard deviation — so $\nu=N-3=11-3=8$. **A** forgets every subtraction. **B** subtracts only the $-1$ for the total-count constraint. **D** subtracts the $-1$ plus one parameter, forgetting that *both* $m$ and $s$ were fitted. Each fitted parameter removes one more degree of freedom.
practice.theory: For a normality $\chi^2$ with mean and st. dev. estimated from the data, $\nu=(\text{categories})-3$.

practice.prompt: A two-sided $\chi^2$ normality test at $P=85\%$ gives boundaries $3.14$ and $14.27$ ($\nu=8$). The experimental statistic is $W=10.94$. The conclusion is:

options.content: Do not reject normality — $W$ lies inside $[3.14,\,14.27]$
Reject normality — $W$ is too small (fit too perfect)
The test is invalid; $\chi^2$ cannot be applied two-sided

practice.explanation: Since $3.14<10.94<14.27$, $W$ falls inside the acceptance band, so the hypothesis of a normal distribution is **not rejected** (the test provides no evidence of a systematic effect). **A** and **C** misplace $W$ relative to the band. **D** is wrong: the course runs the $\chi^2$ exactly two-sided — a $W$ far below the lower boundary would itself be suspicious (an unrealistically perfect fit).
practice.theory: A two-sided $\chi^2$ band rejects both an implausibly bad fit ($W$ too high) and an implausibly perfect one ($W$ too low).

practice.prompt: On a Normal Probability Plot, the $i$-th ordered value is given which plotting position before converting to a quantile?
practice.explanation: The plotting position is $f_{rc,i}=(i-0.5)/n$ and the theoretical quantile is $z_i=\mathrm{NORM.S.INV}(f_{rc,i})$. **A** ($i/n$) sends the largest value to $f=1$, where $z=+\infty$; the $-0.5$ continuity correction exists precisely to avoid that. **B** leaves the rank unscaled, so it is not a probability. **C** confuses the plotting position with the cumulative probability of the data value. Plotting $x_{(i)}$ against $z_i$ gives a straight line when the data are normal.
practice.theory: The $(i-0.5)/n$ correction keeps the extreme order statistics off the $0$/$1$ boundaries where the normal quantile diverges.

practice.prompt: An operator's NPP is steeper than the reference line in the centre and flattens in the tails (a hypernormal shape). The most likely metrological cause is:

options.content: The operator personally discarded the data farthest from the expected value (a filtering process)
A linear drift in time during data collection
Two different instruments were mixed (a discontinuity)
Random noise only; the data are perfectly normal

practice.explanation: A **hypernormal** distribution has too few data in the tails (a bell higher in the centre, lower in the tails). Metrologically this points to a **filtering** process: the operator excluded, on personal judgment, the values deviating most from the expected one, artificially shrinking the tails. **B** and **C** describe *systematic* effects that produce the opposite, **hyponormal**, shape (heavier tails / bimodal). **D** contradicts the observed non-linearity of the NPP.
practice.theory: Hypernormal $\Rightarrow$ depleted tails $\Rightarrow$ filtering. Hyponormal $\Rightarrow$ heavy tails $\Rightarrow$ discontinuities or tendencies (drift).

practice.prompt: Why does the course prefer the IQR / box-plot rule as a *robust* outlier check compared with using $m\pm k\,s$?

options.content: Because $Q_1$ and $Q_3$ ignore the tails, so one extreme point cannot inflate the fences the way it inflates $s$
Because the IQR fences are always wider, so nothing is ever excluded
Because the IQR requires the data to be normal, unlike $m\pm k\,s$
Because $Q_1-1.5\,IQR$ equals $m-2.58\,s$ exactly for any data

practice.explanation: The fences $Q_1-1.5\,IQR$ and $Q_3+1.5\,IQR$ are built from quartiles, which depend only on the central ordering and ignore the extreme tails — so a single wild value cannot move them much, whereas it strongly inflates $m$ and $s$. That is the meaning of **robust**. **B** is false (the rule does flag points). **C** is backwards: the IQR rule is *distribution-free*, needing no normality assumption. **D** is a coincidental relation only near normality ($1.5\,IQR\approx\pm2.7\sigma$), not an identity for arbitrary data.
practice.theory: Robust statistics resist the influence of outliers; quartile-based fences do, moment-based limits ($m,s$) do not.

exam.title: Chauvenet exclusion limits and outlier decision
exam.statement: A set of $n=20$ length readings has sample mean $m=44.90\ \mu\mathrm{m}$ and sample standard deviation $s=3.01\ \mu\mathrm{m}$ (STDEV.S). Apply Chauvenet's criterion to decide whether the value $36\ \mu\mathrm{m}$ is a measurement accident. Report (a) the per-datum risk $\alpha$, (b) the standardized cut $z$, (c) the lower and upper exclusion limits, and (d) the Yes/No decision for $36\ \mu\mathrm{m}$.

steps.title: Per-datum risk
steps.content: $\alpha=\dfrac{1}{2n}=\dfrac{1}{40}=0.025$ (a probability, not 2.5 % reported as a percentage). Each tail carries $\dfrac{1}{4n}=\dfrac{1}{80}=0.0125$.

steps.title: Decision
steps.content: $36\ \mu\mathrm{m}<x_{li}=38.16\ \mu\mathrm{m}$, so it lies in the lower tail beyond the limit: it **is** an outlier. Decision: **Yes**, the outlier value is $36$.

exam.tips: Report $\alpha$ as a probability (0.025), never a percentage, and always use STDEV.S for $s$. Compute the limits once from the *raw* sample; only after deciding do you recompute $m$ and $s$ on the survivors for downstream steps.

exam.title: Chi-square normality test: statistic, dof and decision
exam.statement: A frequency distribution of $n=50$ data uses $N=11$ classes. The per-class contributions $(f_a-f_t)^2/f_t$ sum to $W=10.94$. The normal used to compute the theoretical counts has the sample mean and sample standard deviation. Test normality two-sided at confidence $P=85\%$, given the $\chi^2$ boundaries $3.14$ (lower) and $14.27$ (upper). (a) State the degrees of freedom. (b) Place $W$ relative to the band. (c) Give the conclusion and what it means physically.
exam.given: $N=11$ classes, $W=10.94$; mean and st. dev. fitted from data; $P=85\%$ ($\alpha=15\%$); boundaries $\chi^2_{low}=3.14$, $\chi^2_{up}=14.27$.

steps.title: Degrees of freedom
steps.content: Three constraints are imposed (total area, mean, standard deviation), so $\nu=N-3=11-3=8$.

steps.title: Compare with the two-sided band
steps.content: The acceptance band at $P=85\%$ is $[3.14,\,14.27]$. Since $3.14<W=10.94<14.27$, the statistic falls **inside** the band.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: The null hypothesis (data are normal) is **not rejected** at $P=85\%$. The test provides no evidence of a systematic effect or measurement accident large enough to deform the distribution — although disturbances too small to detect may still be present.

exam.finalAnswer: $\nu=8$; $W=10.94$ lies inside $[3.14,\,14.27]$; normality is **not rejected** at $P=85\%$ — no detectable systematic distortion.
exam.tips: The most common errors: (1) using $\nu=N-1$ instead of $N-3$ (you fitted both $m$ and $s$); (2) doing a one-sided test — the course rejects both too-large and too-small $W$. A $W$ below $3.14$ would mean a suspiciously perfect fit, not a good one.
